---
title: "`BayesBrainMap` step 1 demo: population-derived priors"
author: "Nohelia Da Silva Sanchez, Diego Derman, Saige Rutherford, Damon D. Pham, Ellyn Butler, Mary Beth Nebel, Amanda F. Mejia"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    toc: false
    number_sections: false
  html_document: default
bibliography: references.bib
citeproc: true
link-citations: yes
header-includes: \usepackage{pifont} \usepackage{float} \usepackage{caption}
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, fig.pos = "H")
library(kableExtra)
library(magrittr)
library(knitr)
library(BayesBrainMap)
library(ciftiTools)
wb_path <- "C:/Users/diego/Downloads/workbench-windows64-v2.1.0/workbench/bin_windows64"
ciftiTools.setOption("wb_path", wb_path)
#library(osfr)
#osf_repo <- osf_retrieve_node("k6vx8")

# setup environment
dir_project <- file.path(getwd(), "..") # Path to GitHub folder
dir_data <- file.path(dir_project, "data")
dir_manuscript <- file.path(dir_project, "manuscript")

## Start definitions 
# precalculated priors URL look-up table
prior_url_lookup <- list(
  Yeo17 = "https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/u2r6e",
  GICA15 = "https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/2dk58",
  GICA25 = "https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/2umyv",
  MSC = "https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/7knmf",
  PROFUMO = "https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/vsxg7"
)

# Default mode main component idx per template (for visualization purposes)
# This can be modified to visualize other networks/components as desired.
default_idx <- list(
  Yeo17 = 14, # DefaultA
  GICA15 = 2, #
  GICA25 = 3, # 
  MSC = 1,    # 
  PROFUMO = 1 # 
)

# phase encoding direction for BOLD in population priors
phe = "combined"

```

## Bayesian Brain Mapping Overview

This demo accompanies the manuscript "Bayesian brain mapping: population-informed individualized functional topography and connectivity" ([Da Silva Sanchez et al., 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.01551v1)), which provides a comprehensive introduction to Bayesian Brain Mapping (BBM).
Briefly, there are two steps to performing BBM, both implemented in the `BayesBrainMap` R package: *(1) prior estimation* and *(2) model fitting*.

In this demo we will walk through the code for the first step. If you aim to download a pre-calculated prior based on the HCP population, or already estimated your own prior, you can skip to `demo_step2` to see how to fit the model and estimate subject-level brain maps.

The code shown within this demo is intended for learning purposes, and is not intended to be run within this demo due to computational resources and data availability (we cannot share the HCP data ourselves).
However, we do provide instructions for running all steps yourself and the goal is to teach you how to do so.

```{r model-overview, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap="Overview of Bayesian Brain Mapping. The two main steps are (1) prior estimation and (2) model fitting.", message=TRUE, warning=TRUE, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_manuscript, "Figure1", "Figure1.png"))
```

## Setup

To reproduce this workflow, first clone the repository to your local machine or cluster:

```{bash eval=FALSE}
git clone https://github.com/mandymejia/BBM-priors.git # if you are running this file, you have already done this step
cd BBM-priors/
```

This GitHub repository contains the priors for the Yeo17 parcellation.
If you wish to use another parcellation (i.e., GICA, MSC, PROFUMO) they can be downloaded from [OSF](https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/osfstorage).
Once you have downloaded additional priors from OSF, they should be moved into the `priors/` folder within the data `folder/` to match the structure shown below.

The folder structure should look like this, including a `data/templates` directory containing hard parcellations, continuous networks maps, to be used as templates for estiamting priors. The `data/posteriors` directory will contain the subject-level brain maps estimated in the model fitting step, and the `data/priors` directory will contain the population-level priors estimated in the prior estimation step (see Figure).

```{=html}
<pre style="font-family: monospace; white-space: pre;">
BBM-priors/
├── data/
│   ├── templates/
│   ├── posteriors/
│   └── priors/
│       └────  Yeo17/
│       └────  ...
├── src/
│   ├── 00_main.R
│   ├── 0_setup.R
│   ├── 1_fd_time_filtering.R
│   └── ...
├── demo/
│   └── BBM-demo.Rmd
└── ...
</pre>
```

This section initializes the environment by loading required packages, setting analysis parameters, and defining directory paths.

**Important:** Before running the workflow, you must review `0_setup.R` and install any necessary packages, ensure you have an installation of Connectome Workbench, and update the following variables to match your local or cluster environment:

-   `dir_project` (path to the GitHub folder)

-   `dir_train` (path to the HCP data)

-   `HCP_unrestricted_fname` (path to the unrestricted HCP CSV if you have access to it)

-   `HCP_restricted_fname` (path to the restricted HCP CSV if you have access to it)

-   `wb_path` (location of the CIFTI Workbench on your system)

```{r, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='hide', eval=FALSE}
github_repo_dir <- getwd()
src_dir <- file.path(github_repo_dir, "../src")
#source(file.path(src_dir, "setup.R"))
```

## Prior Estimation with `estimate_prior()`:

### Choosing Training Subjects

1.  **Filter Subjects by Sufficient fMRI Scan Duration**

    See [Appendix A.1](#appendix-filtering-1) and script: `1_fd_time_filtering.R`

2.  **Filter Unrelated Subjects**

    See [Appendix A.2](#appendix-filtering-2) and script: `2_unrelated_filtering.R`

3.  **Balance sex within age groups**

    See [Appendix A.3](#appendix-filtering-3) and script: `3_balance_age_sex.R`

The resulting subject list (`valid_combined_subjects_balanced.rds`) is used throughout the rest of the analysis.

### Run `estimate_prior()`

In this step, we estimate group-level statistical priors using the `estimate_prior()` function from the `BayesBrainMap` package.
We must specify the training data, processing choices, and the template file.

#### Training Data

The final lists of subjects saved above (`valid_combined_subjects_balanced.rds`) includes individuals who passed motion filtering in both LR and RL directions for both sessions, were unrelated, and were sex-balanced within age groups.

#### Preprocessing Parameters

To standardize scan duration and improve data quality, we apply both initial volume censoring and temporal truncation using parameters handled directly by the `estimate_prior()` function from the `BayesBrainMap` package.

Specifically:

-   `drop_first = 15` removes the first 15 volumes from each scan to eliminate early signal instability and motion artifacts.

-   `scrub` defines volumes to censor and exclude after a target duration.
    We truncate data to the first 10 minutes (600 seconds), excluding any volumes beyond that point.

-   `hpf = 0.01` applies a high-pass filter at 0.01 Hz to remove low-frequency drift from the signal.

See [Appendix B](#appendix-scrub) for more details.

#### Template File

Here we use the Yeo17 parcellation [@yeo2011organization] as the template.
For details on how this parcellation was processed and simplified for use, see [Appendix C](#appendix-parcellations).

#### Run `estimate_prior()`

**Note:** The code below is provided for reference but is not evaluated in this demo.
To run it yourself, you must have access to HCP data and update `dir_train` with the path to your HCP data directory.
Pre-computed priors are available for download from [OSF](https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/osfstorage) and are used in the model fitting section below.

Running `estimate_prior()` on the full subject list (\~350 subjects) takes approximately 27 hours and uses 6 GB of memory when run sequentially. 
With parallel processing enabled via `usePar`, runtime is significantly reduced. Using 48 concurrent threads, prior were estimated in 2 hours with a peak memory usage of 126 GB.

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}

# Read in template
Yeo17 <- readRDS(file.path(dir_data, "templates", "Yeo17_simplified_mwall.rds"))
# Exclude 0 or -1 -> medial wall
valid_keys <- Yeo17$meta$cifti$labels[[1]]$Key
inds <- valid_keys[valid_keys > 0]

#point to training data
BOLD1_fname <- "MNINonLinear/Results/rfMRI_REST1_LR/rfMRI_REST1_LR_Atlas_MSMAll_hp2000_clean.dtseries.nii"
BOLD2_fname <- "MNINonLinear/Results/rfMRI_REST1_RL/rfMRI_REST1_RL_Atlas_MSMAll_hp2000_clean.dtseries.nii"
BOLD1_paths <- file.path(dir_train, final_subject_ids, BOLD1_fname)
BOLD2_paths <- file.path(dir_train, final_subject_ids, BOLD2_fname)

#run estimate_prior
prior <- estimate_prior(
        BOLD = BOLD1_paths,
        BOLD2 = BOLD2_paths,
        template = Yeo17,
        GSR = FALSE,
        TR = 0.72,
        hpf = 0.01,
        Q2 = 0,
        Q2_max = NULL,
        verbose = TRUE,
        inds = inds,
        brainstructures = c("left", "right"),
        drop_first = 15,
        scrub = scrub,
        usePar=usePar,
        wb_path = wb_path
    )
        
# Save file
saveRDS(prior, file.path(dir_prior, 'prior.rds'))
```

## Prior Visualization

In this section, we visualize the Yeo17 template and the pre-estimated prior maps (mean and variance).
We also visualize the functional connectivity prior.

First, we load the pre-estimated prior that will be used for visualization and model fitting.
This file is included in the *data_OSF* repository under `priors/Yeo17/` and can be loaded after downloading from OSF — no need to re-run `estimate_prior()`:

```{r load-prior, eval=FALSE}
################################################################################
# Select tempalte to use for the remainder of this demo
template <- "Yeo17" # options: "Yeo17", "GICA15", "GICA25", "MSC", "PROFUMO"
gsr_mode <- "noGSR" # options: "GSR", "noGSR"
################################################################################

osf_url <- prior_url_lookup[[template]]

# check if file exists. If not, download from OSF and load it. If it does, just load it.
prior_fname <- file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode,".rds"))

if (!file.exists(prior_fname)) {
  message("Prior file not found locally. Downloading from OSF...")
  osf_tibble <- osf_download(osf_retrieve_file(osf_url), path = dirname(prior_fname), verbose = FALSE, progress = TRUE, conflicts = "skip")
} else {
  message("Prior file found locally. Loading...")
}

# regardless, load the prior file
prior <- readRDS(prior_fname)
```

### Priors for Spatial Engagement

The Yeo17 parcellation divides the cortex into 17 functional networks.
See [Appendix C](#appendix-parcellations) for details on how this parcellation was prepared.
The `05_visualization_prior.R` script in `src/` loads each estimated prior file and plots both the mean and standard deviation.

**The code below is shown for reference only.** Pre-computed plot images are included in the repository under `priors/priors_plots`, so you do not need to run this code — the figures are displayed directly below.

```{r plot-prior, eval=FALSE}

  # If Yeo17 template, template_parc_table needs to be updated to only reflect the correct number of labels (17)
  if (template == "Yeo17") {
    prior$template_parc_table <- subset(prior$template_parc_table, prior$template_parc_table$Key > 0)
  }

    # Plot mean
    plot(
      prior,
      stat = "mean",
      fname = file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_maps", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_DefaultA.png")),
      idx = default_idx[[template]],
      title = paste0(template, "Network Default (#", as.character(default_idx[[template]]),") (mean)")
    )

    # Plot standard deviation
    plot(
      prior,
      stat = "sd",
      fname = file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_maps", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_DefaultA.png")),
      idx = default_idx[[template]],
      title = paste0(template, "Network Default (#", as.character(default_idx[[template]]),") (sqrt varNN)")
    )
```

```{r model-overview2, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', message=TRUE, warning=TRUE, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_maps", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_DefaultA_mean.png")))
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_maps", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_DefaultA_sd.png")))
```

### Priors for Functional Connectivity

The functional connectivity (FC) prior captures the expected correlation structure between networks.
The `src/` folder contains visualization scripts for FC priors using both Cholesky and Inverse-Wishart parameterizations.

This step visualizes the Functional Connectivity (FC) prior using both the Cholesky and Inverse-Wishart parameterizations.
For the group-level prior, we compute and plot:

-   Mean FC matrix (off-diagonal values only)

-   Standard deviation of FC estimates (from the variance matrix)

The plots are saved in: `data/priors/*template_name*/plots_FC/` with filenames indicating the template, GSR mode, and estimation method.

```{r plot-FC, eval=FALSE}

  # If Yeo17 template, template_parc_table needs to be updated to only reflect the correct number of labels (17)
  if (template == "Yeo17") {
    prior$template_parc_table <- subset(prior$template_parc_table, prior$template_parc_table$Key > 0)
  }

    # Plot mean
    plot(
      prior,
      stat = "mean",
      fname = file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_FC", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_FC_Cholesky_mean.png")),
      idx = default_idx[[template]],
      title = paste0(template, "Network Default (#", as.character(default_idx[[template]]),") (mean)")
    )

    # Plot standard deviation
    plot(
      prior,
      stat = "sd",
      fname = file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_FC", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_FC_Cholesky_sd.png")),
      idx = default_idx[[template]],
      title = paste0(template, "Network Default (#", as.character(default_idx[[template]]),") (sqrt varNN)")
    )
```

#### PNG imgaes (4 per prior)

-   `Yeo17_FC_Cholesky_mean.png`

-   `Yeo17_FC_Cholesky_sd.png`

-   `Yeo17_FC_InverseWishart_mean.png`

-   `Yeo17_FC_InverseWishart_sd.png`

These visualizations allow for a direct comparison of spatial FC structure and uncertainty across priors and estimation methods.

The figures below show the mean and standard deviation of FC priors using Cholesky and Inverse-Wishart methods.

```{r model-overview3, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', message=TRUE, warning=TRUE, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_FC", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_FC_Cholesky_mean.png")))
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_FC", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_FC_Cholesky_sd.png")))
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_FC", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_FC_IW_mean.png")))
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_data, "priors", template, "plots_FC", paste0("prior_", phe, "_", template, "_", gsr_mode, "_FC_IW_sd.png")))
```

## Appendix A: Subject Filtering {#appendix-subjectfiltering}

### Appendix A.1: Filter Subjects by Sufficient fMRI Scan Duration {#appendix-filtering-1}

We begin by filtering subjects based on the fMRI scan duration after motion scrubbing For each subject, and for each session (`REST1`, `REST2`) and encoding direction (`LR`, `RL`), we compute framewise displacement (FD) using the `fMRIscrub` package.
We use a lagged and filtered version of FD [@pham2023less] [@power2012spurious] [@fair2009functional] appropriate for multiband data.
FD is calculated from the `Movement_Regressors.txt` file available in the HCP data for each subject, encoding and session.

A volume is considered valid if it passes an FD threshold, and a subject is retained only if both sessions in both encodings have at least 10 minutes (600 seconds) of valid data.

The final subject lists include those who passed the filtering criteria separately for each encoding: `LR`, `RL`, and their intersection, referred to as the`combined` list.
The combined list includes only subjects who passed all criteria for both LR and RL encodings across both visits (REST1 and REST2), and is the one used throughout this project.

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# This script filters subjects based on motion using framewise displacement (FD)
# from fMRIscrub.
# For each subject, encoding (LR/RL), and session (REST1/REST2), it computes FD,
# flags volumes exceeding the FD threshold of 0.5 mm (scrubbing), 
# and calculates valid scan time after excluding those high-motion volumes.
# Subjects with ≥10 minutes of valid data in both sessions are retained.
# Outputs (saved in dir_results):
# - Valid subject lists for LR, RL, and combined encodings (intersection)
# - FD summary per subject/session/encoding

#set up path, etc.
github_repo_dir <- getwd()
src_dir <- file.path(github_repo_dir, "src")
source(file.path(src_dir, "0_setup.R"))

# Run script to exclude sessions based on head motion
source(file.path(src_dir, "1_fd_time_filtering.R"))
```

During this step, an FD summary table is generated with the following columns:

-   subject: HCP subject ID

-   session: REST1 or REST2

-   encoding: LR or RL

-   mean_fd: mean framewise displacement

-   valid_time_sec: total duration of valid data in seconds

#### Preview of FD Summary Table

```{r results='asis', echo=FALSE}
fd_summary <- read.csv(file.path(dir_data, "priors", "filtering", "fd_summary.csv"))

kable(fd_summary[1:4, ]) %>%
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, position = "center")
```

As shown above, subject 100206 qualifies for further analysis because each of the four sessions (REST1/REST2 × LR/RL) contains at least 600 seconds of valid data.

The script is currently designed to filter based on valid time only, but it can be easily adapted to apply additional constraints such as maximum mean FD thresholds if desired (e.g., mean_fd \< 0.1).

### Appendix A.2: Filter Unrelated Subjects {#appendix-filtering-2}

Building on the previous step, we use the HCP restricted demographic data to exclude related individuals.
This step helps ensure the statistical independence of subjects in the group-level priors estimation.

For the `LR`, `RL`, and `combined` lists of valid subjects derived in the previous step, we:

1.  Subset the HCP restricted demographics to include only those subjects with at least 10 minutes remaining after scrubbing.

2.  Filter by `Family_ID` to retain a single individual per family.

Note: This step requires access to the HCP restricted data.
If you do not have access, you can skip this step, resulting in some related subjects being included in your training data.

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# This script filters subjects to retain only unrelated individuals, using Family ID 
# information from the restricted HCP data.
# For each encoding (LR, RL, combined), it selects one subject per family from the 
# FD-valid lists.
# Outputs (saved in dir_personal due to restricted data):
# - Unrelated subject lists for LR, RL, and combined encodings (intersection)

source(file.path(src_dir, "2_unrelated_filtering.R"))
```

### Appendix A.3: Balance Sex Within Age Groups {#appendix-filtering-3}

In the final step of subject selection, we balance sex across age groups to reduce potential demographic bias in priors estimation.

For the `LR`, `RL`, and `combined` lists of valid subjects derived in the previous step, we:

-   Subset the HCP unrestricted demographics to include only those subjects.

-   Split subjects by age group and examine the sex distribution within each group.

-   If both sexes are present but imbalanced, we randomly remove subjects from the overrepresented group to achieve balance.

Note: If the unrelated subject filtering step is skipped (e.g., due to lack of restricted data access), the code automatically falls back to using `valid_<encoding>_subjects_FD` instead of `valid_<encoding>_subjects_unrelated`.

The final list of valid subjects is saved in `dir_results` as:

-   `valid_<encoding>_subjects_balanced.csv`

-   `valid_<encoding>_subjects_balanced.rds` (used in the prior estimation step)

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# This script balances sex within each age group for subjects who passed FD and 
# unrelated filtering.
# For each encoding (LR, RL, combined), it samples subjects to equalize the number
# of males and females per age group, 
# unless an age group includes only one gender (in which case no balancing is applied).
# Uses age and gender information from the unrestricted HCP data.
# Outputs (saved in dir_personal):
# - Sex-balanced subject lists for LR, RL, and combined encodings (as .csv and .rds)

source(file.path(src_dir, "3_balance_age_sex.R"))
```

## Appendix B: Scrubbing and Temporal Truncation {#appendix-scrub}

Given the HCP TR of 0.72 seconds, 10 minutes corresponds to:

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
T_total <- floor(600 / TR_HCP) # ~833 volumes
```

To define the volumes to scrub (i.e., exclude beyond 10 minutes), we compute:

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
T_scrub_start <- T_total + 1
scrub_BOLD1 <- replicate(length(BOLD_paths1), T_scrub_start:nT_HCP, simplify = FALSE)
scrub_BOLD2 <- replicate(length(BOLD_paths2), T_scrub_start:nT_HCP, simplify = FALSE)
scrub <- list(scrub_BOLD1, scrub_BOLD2)
```

Because `drop_first = 15` removes frames before truncation, the final retained time series per scan will be slightly shorter than 10 minutes.
Approximately:

```         
(833 - 15) * 0.72 = ~589 seconds (~9.8 minutes)
```

## Appendix C: Prepare Yeo17 Parcellation for Prior Estimation {#appendix-parcellations}

In this step, we load and preprocess a group-level cortical parcellation to be used as the template to estimate the priors in the next step.
Specifically, we use the Yeo 17-network parcellation (`Yeo_17`) and perform the following operations:

-   Simplify the labels by collapsing hemisphere-specific naming and removing subnetwork identifiers, grouping regions by their main network.

-   Create a new `dlabel` object that maps each vertex to its corresponding network.

-   Mask out the medial wall to exclude it from analysis.

The resulting parcellation is saved as `Yeo17_simplified_mwall.rds`.

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# This script simplifies the Yeo 17-network parcellation by collapsing region 
# labels and masking out the medial wall.
# It creates a cleaned version of the parcellation suitable for downstream analyses.
# Output:
# - Saved as RDS file in dir_data: "Yeo17_simplified_mwall.rds"

source(file.path(src_dir, "4_parcellations.R"))
```

We can visualize the Yeo17 networks and their corresponding labels:

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# Load libraries
library(ciftiTools)
library(rgl)
rgl::setupKnitr()

# Load the parcellation
yeo17 <- readRDS(file.path(dir_data, "outputs", "Yeo17_simplified_mwall.rds"))
yeo17 <- add_surf(yeo17)

view_xifti_surface(
  xifti = yeo17,
  widget = TRUE,
  title = "Yeo17 Network Parcellation",
  legend_ncol = 6,
  legend_fname = file.path(dir_manuscript, "parcellations_plots", "Yeo17", "Yeo17_legend.png"),
  fname=file.path(dir_data, "outputs", "parcellations_plots", "Yeo17")
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="60%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_manuscript, "parcellations_plots", "Yeo17", "Yeo17.png"))
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, out.width="80%", fig.align="center"}
# Show legend
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_manuscript, "parcellations_plots", "Yeo17", "Yeo17_legend.png"))
```

## Appendix D: Example Function Call for Prior Estimation {#appendix-example}

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# For detailed parameter descriptions, run: ?estimate_prior

estimate_prior(
  BOLD = BOLD_paths1,         # REST1 LR scans (list of file paths)
  BOLD2 = BOLD_paths2,        # REST2 LR scans (same subjects/order as BOLD)
  template = GICA,            # GICA 15-component parcellation (CIFTI dscalar file path)
  GSR = TRUE,                 # Apply global signal regression
  TR = 0.72,                  # Repetition time in seconds
  hpf = 0.01,                 # High-pass filter cutoff in Hz
  Q2 = 0,                     # No nuisance IC denoising
  drop_first = 15,            # Drop first 15 volumes
  scrub = scrub,              # Timepoints to scrub (list format)
  verbose = TRUE              # Print progress updates
)
```

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
